Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time on weekends, but it rarely worked out the way I planned. Most of my studying happened in bursts when I felt the pressure building up. I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, which helped since I learn better by hearing stuff. With only about 12 hours a week to study and four online courses, I had to spread myself thin.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due. I'd have quiet weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be cramming because multiple deadlines were approaching. Regular times just didn't happen for me—I don't have the luxury of a perfect schedule when resources are tight and life keeps throwing curveballs. I tried to be consistent but it never stuck.
Q3: Usually it was because something was due or I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes a classmate would message me about an assignment and that would remind me to log in. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around—there had to be a reason, like checking grades or finding out what I'd missed. Panic was honestly a big motivator.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a few weeks where I'd check in more regularly, but it never became automatic for me. When things got stressful or I fell behind, the LMS started feeling like this thing I was avoiding rather than something helpful. I never got to that point where it was just part of my daily habits.
Q5: The audio recordings and video lectures were the most useful for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them while commuting or doing chores, which made studying possible when I couldn't sit down with a textbook. The discussion forums were also helpful because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. Those peer interactions helped clarify things.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. Reading takes me a lot longer and I just couldn't keep up with everything. The optional quizzes I'd put off until I forgot about them entirely. When you're juggling four courses with limited time and energy, something has to give, and usually it was the stuff that wasn't directly graded.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd do it. Also, anything that was explained verbally or through video felt more accessible to me. When something was just a wall of text with no clear payoff, I'd skip it or skim through quickly.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required activities had consequences, so I prioritized those even when I was exhausted. Optional activities felt like they were designed for students with more time and fewer worries than me. I'd tell myself I'd get to them eventually, but eventually never came because there was always something more urgent.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download the audio files and listen to them on my phone when I didn't have internet or was traveling. I'd also take handwritten notes from lectures and study from those instead of going back to the LMS. Sometimes I'd screenshot important slides so I could review them without logging in. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were many times I was studying from my notes or listening to downloaded recordings, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also discuss course material with classmates in person or over text, working through problems together. The LMS would just show me as inactive during those times, but I was actually engaged with the content in my own way.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once, maybe pause it a few times to write things down, then study from my notes for days afterward. Or I'd download a lecture recording and replay it multiple times on my phone. The LMS would only register that initial access, not the hours I spent with that material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms everything got really hard. I was stressed, not sleeping well, and the workload from all four courses piled up at once. My motivation just tanked and I started avoiding the LMS because logging in made me anxious about everything I was behind on. The CBT session I had helped a bit, but bouncing back was slow with my low resilience.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities—like work and family stuff—meant I couldn't always engage when I wanted to. The confusion in some course materials made it worse because I'd spend time trying to understand something and then run out of energy for the rest.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open videos or readings but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about other problems in my life or just too tired to focus. I'd technically be 'watching' something but not actually learning. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than real studying.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was anxiety—starting something felt overwhelming, so I'd avoid it until I couldn't anymore. The stress would build up and make it even harder to begin. It's a cycle I recognized but couldn't always break out of, especially with my low resilience to setbacks.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd be pretty inactive until a deadline was approaching, then I'd suddenly be all over the LMS, downloading materials, checking requirements, posting questions. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I could function with everything else going on. The urgency was what got me moving.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback on assignments, it did motivate me to engage more. Seeing that my work scored well—like those 90+ scores on assignments—made me feel like my effort was worth it. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose that momentum. Quick, clear feedback kept me connected to the course.
Q18: The course structure was okay but having four online courses at once made it hard to keep track of everything. When modules were clearly organized with audio components, I could follow along better. But when the structure was confusing or things were scattered across different sections, I'd get lost and disengage. Clear weekly expectations would have helped me a lot.
Q19: Having video and audio options made engagement easier for me since that's how I learn best. But the amount of reading required was a barrier—I just couldn't keep up with it all. Also, when deadlines for different courses overlapped, it made everything harder. More flexibility in due dates would have helped students like me who are juggling a lot.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure out what they need to do but feel overwhelmed about actually doing it. They might be searching for information, re-reading instructions, or just anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. It's like being stuck in preparation mode without being able to move forward to execution.
Q21: That actually sounds like me to some extent. My attendance was low and my LMS activity wasn't super high, but I still passed and did well on assignments. I studied a lot offline, listened to recordings on my phone, and learned from peers outside the platform. The LMS doesn't capture all the ways someone can actually engage with course material.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't measure understanding or effort. They miss offline studying, peer discussions, and all the thinking you do away from the screen. They also can't tell if someone was genuinely engaged or just had the page open while doing something else. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks for grades. It's when you're curious about what you're learning and making connections to other things. For me, engagement is when I'm actively listening and thinking, even if I'm not clicking around on a platform. It's a mental state more than an activity count.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures on my phone during commutes, or the study sessions with classmates where we worked through problems together. It wouldn't capture my anxiety about logging in or how the CBT session helped me manage my stress. It also wouldn't show that despite my low attendance and moderate activity, I was genuinely trying to learn and pass my courses.
